rulings.Case Title: PYONG K. CHIN VS. DEPARTMENT OF INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS, DIVISION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Mandatory Dismissal Based Upon Failure To Prosecute Or, In The Alternative, Discretionary Dismissal
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 27, 2018, Line 1, RESPONDENT DEPARTMENT OF INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS, DIVISION OF LABOR STANDARDS ENFORCEMENT'S Motion For Mandatory Dismissal Based Upon Failure To Prosecute Or, In The Alternative, Discretionary Dismissal. Respondent Department of Industrial Relations, Division of Labor Standards Enforcement's motion for mandatory dismissal based upon failure to prosecute is granted. No opposition filed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the court will sign the proposed order submitted with the moving papers. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: ALLAN QUITON VS. BRIAN MCWILLIAMS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants' Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Admissions, Set No. 2 And To Form Interrogatories, Set No. 4 (Interrogatory No. 17.1); Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 27, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANTS BRIAN MCWILLIAMS, POTS 'N WATER, LLC'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Admissions, Set No. 2 And To Form Interrogatories, Set No. 4 (Interrogatory No. 17.1); Request For Sanctions (part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Court finds that questionable objections have been interposed in plaintiff's discovery responses but that reasonably complete responses, as opposed to objections, have nevertheless been given to most of the inquiries either initially or by amended responses. Moreover, it appears to the Court that plaintiff's deposition has already provided adequate information concerning a portion of the topics inquired about. The Court further finds the motion papers of both parties to be complex, confusing and incomplete. Further, the parties have incorporated discussions and arguments over many unrelated issues. In order to resolve these discovery disputes in a just, speedy and economical manner, the Court makes the following tentative rulings, subject to oral argument if properly requested: Motion to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Admissions - Strike the Preliminary Statement; - As to RFA no. 4, strike the objections and grant a further response which shall be directly responsive as to plaintiff's status as of 2010; - As to RFAs nos. 6, 12, 14, 31, 33, 35, 36, 45, 46, 49, 50, and 57, strike the objections; - As to RFAs nos. 59 and 60, strike the objections and grant further responses which shall be directly responsive; - Otherwise denied; sanctions denied. Motion to Compel Further Responses to Form Interrogatory 17.1 - Strike the Preliminary Objections; - As to the response to RFA no. 6, grant a further response which shall be directly responsive and state requested details in full; - As to the responses to RFAs nos. 29, 30, 31, 33, 35, 36, 43, 45, 46, 49, 50, and 57, strike the objections; - As to the responses to RFAs nos. 59 and 60, strike the objections and grant further responses which shall be directly responsive; - Otherwise denied; sanctions denied. (End of part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE VS. KINK.COM ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Witness Angel Anders Answers To Deposition Questions And Further Deposition As To All Related Follow-Up Questions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 27, 2018, Line 7, PLAINTIFF JOHN DOE'S Motion To Compel Witness Angel Anders' Answers To Deposition Questions And Further Deposition As To All Related Follow-Up Questions. Plaintiff John Doe's motion to compel witness Angel Anders' answers to deposition questions is granted. The deposition questions are directly related to plaintiff's claims and are not absolutely barred by either Health and Safety Code 120975 or constitutional or common law privacy rights. Due to plaintiff's critical need for the information sought by the questions and the lack of any other way to obtain the information, the witness' privacy rights in the information, while significant, do not outweigh the plaintiff's need for the information. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: JIAN WANG ET AL VS. PEI OPERATIONS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Pei Operations, Inc. And City Central Services, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 27, 2018, Line 2, Defendants Pei Operations, Inc. And City Central Services, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). While there is a variation between the two pieces of evidence, the variation does not make this evidence inadmissible and it is for the trier of fact to weigh the differences and determine whether any such statement was made and its significance. Based on the statement alone, if it is believed (and much of defendants' arguments in effect ask this court to disbelieve that the statement was made), a reasonable trier of fact could determine that defendants terminated the employment of all four plaintiffs based on impermissible discrimination. Defendants' effort to invoke the federal stray remarks doctrine is unavailing for two reasons. First, that doctrine has been rejected by the California Supreme in Reid v. Google, Inc., (2010) 24 Cal.4th 512, 538-45. Second, that doctrine only applies to circumstantial evidence, not, as here, direct evidence that an adverse employment action was taken for an impermissible reason. (Id.). Plaintiffs' "withdrew" their harassment and IIED claims and did not oppose the motion as to those claims. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: JIAN WANG ET AL VS. PEI OPERATIONS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 27, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANTS PEI OPERATIONS, INC. AND CITY CENTRAL SERVICES, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (PART 1 of 2 for purposes of entry of tentative ruling only). Defendants City Central Services, Inc. and PEI Operations, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendants City Central Services, Inc. and PEI Operations, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied and their alternate motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the first, third and fourth causes of action for race discrimination, failure to prevent discrimination, and wrongful termination in violation of public policy and granted as to second cause of action for harassment and the fifth cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. There is a triable dispute whether plaintiffs were competently performing their jobs and thus plaintiffs satisfied their "nononerous" burden of showing they have a prima facie case. (Hersant v. Dept. of Social Services (1997) 57 Cal.App.4th 997, 1005 (describing the prima facie case as "nononerous".)). Plaintiffs show that they worked at Crunch Gym for years without complaint. (Quan Depo. 8:2-12; 27:19-28:2; Xu Depo. 9:2-11; 40:2-41:22; Chen Depo. 9:20-25; 50:7-51:1; Wang Depo, 9:10-10:17; 36:8-23.). While defendants have shown they had a legitimate reason to terminate the plaintiffs' employment because of plaintiffs' asserted sub-standard performance and the complaints of Crunch Gym, there is direct evidence that plaintiffs' employment was terminated due to impermissible discriminatory animus. Defendants' fact 48 cites Mr. Quan's interrogatory response where he was told that "We don't want you Chinese here." Defendants argue that the interrogatory response differs from his deposition testimony. (Continued in Part 2 of 2) =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Travelers Motion To Strike Altamont Electrics Cross Complaint, Memo Of P&A, Proof Of Service
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 27, 2018, Line 3, INTERVENOR THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY OF CONNECTICUT'S Motion To Strike Altamont Electrics Cross Complaint. Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company of Connecticut's anti-SLAPP motion to strike the entirety of the cross-complaint filed by Altamont Electric Co. is granted. Travelers satisfied its first prong burden of showing that Altamont's cross-complaint arises from petitioning activity -- specifically, activity pertaining to the settlement of litigation -- protected by the anti-SLAPP statute. Altamont has not satisfied its burden to show an exception to protected activity such as the commercial speech exemption or the public interest exemption. Nor has Altamont satisfied its second prong burden of showing that there is minimal merit to its cross-complaint. Altamont's failure to provide any evidence to show that it can prove its cross-complaint and its sole reliance on the allegations of the cross-complaint precludes it from meeting its second prong burden. Altamont's argument that its allegations show that Travelers committed extortion or other illegal activity and thus its cross-complaint is outside of the anti-SLAPP statute is unavailing. Assertedly "illegal" speech or petitioning is outside the protections of the anti-SLAPP statute only when a defendant either concedes or the evidence conclusively establishes that the defendant's conduct was illegal as a matter of law, and there is neither a concession nor conclusive (nor any) evidence of illegality in this case. (Flatley v. Mauro (2006) 39 Cal. 4th 312, 320). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Travelers is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Court finds Revenue and Taxation Code Section 408 to be ambiguous as applied to previous owners of the subject property, especially corporate owners which have ceased to have any existence thereby eliminating any possibility of consent. In accordance with well-established principles of liberal discovery, practical construction of statutes and avoidance of absurd results, the Court will order production of the records in question and the designation of said records as confidential under the existing protective order in this case. rulings.Case Title: KAREN ELAINE GOODRICH MD VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 27, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Medical Board of California's demurrer to the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Karen Goodrich is sustained without leave to amend. The May 17, 2018 order sustaining the Board's demurrer to the complaint gave Dr. Goodrich leave to amend to allege a viable claim that is not an attack on the Board's March 10, 2014 decision. Dr. Goodrich has not done so in her first amended complaint and it now appears that she is unable to do so. For the most part the first amended complaint is a re-packaging of the legally insufficient and time-barred allegations alleged in the complaint. To the extent that Dr. Goodrich seeks to rely on contract theories, doing so is an impermissible end run around the rules established for challenging decisions of the Board. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Board is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: TIAN ZHANG VS. APEX CONSTRUCTION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 27, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANT BOSCO LAI'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant Bosco Lai's demurrer to both causes of action in the complaint filed by plaintiff Tian Zhang is sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to both causes of action. The complaint fails to allege defendant Apex Construction's form of business entity. If it is alleged to be a sole proprietorship, then Mr. Lai is legally liable for all of its actions and inactions. If it is alleged to be an entity with separate legal significance, then Mr. Lai is legally liable for its actions and inactions only to the extent that Mr. Lai is the alter ego of Apex and/or personally committed tortious conduct. The complaint also fails to allege that Mr. Lai is the alter ego of Apex or that Mr. Lai personally committed tortious conduct. The complaint also fails to allege a connection between Apex and Mr. Lai to the premises sufficient to support imposition of premises liability. Mr. Zhang is given leave to remedy these deficiencies if he is able to do so. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Lai is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)
